note: Mathlib/MeasureTheory/OuterMeasure/AE.lean:47 — def ae (μ : F) : Filter α, the filter of co-null sets; docstring literally defines the 'almost everywhere' filter, with notation ∀ᵐ x ∂μ, p x built on it.
verify_note: Decl exists in cache and at cited line; docstring reads verbatim 'The "almost everywhere" filter of co-null sets', matching Wikidata Q1139334's description ('false only on a...set with zero measure') exactly — ae IS the formalization, not a related notion.

note: Mathlib/Data/Nat/PrimeFin.lean:35 — def primeFactors (n : ℕ) : Finset ℕ := n.primeFactorsList.toFinset, docstring 'The prime factors of a natural number as a finset'; mem_primeFactors (line 39) confirms membership ↔ p.Prime ∧ p ∣ n ∧ n ≠ 0.
verify_note: Decl and both cited lines verified verbatim; mem_primeFactors's iff (Prime ∧ dvd ∧ n≠0) is exactly Wikidata Q1137759's description 'prime number dividing an integer' — the Finset is Mathlib's standard extensional formalization of the concept, exact is honest.

note: Mathlib/Probability/BrownianMotion/Basic.lean:303 — structure IsBrownianReal formalizes standard (real-valued) Brownian motion, which is the Hurst-parameter-1/2 special case of fractional Brownian motion; no general fBm (arbitrary Hurst exponent H) construction found in Mathlib.
verify_note: Decl confirmed at cited line — extends IsPreBrownianReal with a.s.-continuous paths, i.e. plain Brownian motion; repo-wide grep for 'FractionalBrownian|fractionalBrownian|Hurst' across mathlib4 returns zero hits, so no exact fBm decl exists and special_case (H=1/2 instance, not the general process) is the honest, non-overclaimed label.

note: Mathlib/RingTheory/DedekindDomain/Ideal/Basic.lean:431 — instance Ideal.uniqueFactorizationMonoid : UniqueFactorizationMonoid (Ideal A) for A a Dedekind domain; this is the modern (Dedekind) generalization of Kummer's 'ideal numbers', which were introduced precisely to restore unique factorization in rings of integers — Mathlib formalizes the general ideal-theoretic successor, not the literal historical ideal-number construction.
verify_note: Decl exists as cited, but Wikidata Q1137087 describes ideal number as a specific algebraic-integer construct (subclass of algebraic integer) that historically PRECEDED and motivated Dedekind's ideal theory, not an object that ideal theory later generalizes — 'generalization' mislabels a historical-precursor relation as a formal specialization one, and the cited decl is a Prop-valued instance/theorem (ideals form a UFM) rather than a definition that could stand in an is-a hierarchy with 'ideal number' at all; no decl in Mathlib formalizes Kummer's ideal numbers themselves.
